column1: In the context of cell biology, what is the primary outcome of mitosis regarding the daughter cells?
column2: Two genetically identical diploid daughter cells.

column1: The specialized cell division process that produces haploid gametes from a diploid cell is known as _____.
During which phase of the cell cycle is chromosomal DNA replicated?
Identify the period that encompasses both nuclear division (mitosis) and cytoplasmic division (cytokinesis).
What protein complexes hold sister chromatids together along their length after DNA replication?
Name the five traditionally defined stages of mitosis in their correct sequence.
column2: Prophase, prometaphase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase.

column1: During which stage of mitosis do the duplicated chromosomes first condense and become visible?
What cytoskeletal structure is responsible for pulling duplicated chromosomes apart during mitosis?
In animal cells, what is the mitotic spindle primarily composed of?
At which stage of mitosis do chromosomes align at the equator of the spindle?
What specific site on a chromosome serves as the attachment point for spindle microtubules?
During anaphase, what event triggers the synchronous separation of sister chromatids?
Which phase of mitosis is characterized by the re-formation of the nuclear envelope around two sets of segregated chromosomes?
The division of the cytoplasm that results in two separate cells is called _____.
In animal cells, what transient structure facilitates cytokinesis?
Which two types of protein filaments primarily compose the contractile ring in animal cells?
What is the primary role of condensins during the onset of M phase?
column2: Helping individual chromatids coil into a compact form.

column1: How many rounds of DNA replication occur during the entire process of meiosis?
How many successive rounds of nuclear division occur during meiosis?
What term describes the pairing of duplicated maternal and paternal homologs during meiotic prophase I?
A structure consisting of four sister chromatids (two duplicated homologous chromosomes) is called a _____.
The process by which segments of DNA are exchanged between non-sister chromatids during meiosis I is known as _____.
What is the physical manifestation of a crossover between homologous chromosomes called?
During anaphase I of meiosis, what specific structures are segregated to opposite poles?
How does the behavior of kinetochores in meiosis I differ from mitosis?
column2: Sister-chromatid kinetochores function as a single unit and attach to the same spindle pole.

column1: What is the major mechanical role of chiasmata during metaphase I?
column2: Holding maternal and paternal homologs together against the pull of the spindle.

column1: In meiosis II, what structures are pulled apart during anaphase?
Why is meiosis I often referred to as 'reduction division'?
column2: It reduces the chromosome number from diploid ($2n$) to haploid ($n$).

column1: If an organism has a haploid number of $n = 3$, how many different gamete combinations can be produced by independent assortment alone?
What term refers to the failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis?
Which medical condition is typically caused by an extra copy of chromosome 21 resulting from nondisjunction?
In human females, at what stage does meiosis I arrest for years before completion?
What is the function of the cell-cycle control system?
column2: To ensure cell cycle events occur in the correct sequence and each process is complete before the next begins.

column1: The transition from G2 to M phase is regulated to ensure that what process is complete?
What type of protein kinases are the core components of the cell-cycle control system?
How does the spindle assembly checkpoint prevent aneuploidy?
column2: It delays anaphase until all chromosomes are properly attached to the mitotic spindle.

column1: During which phase of the cell cycle do centrosomes duplicate?
What happens to the nuclear envelope during prometaphase?
column2: It breaks down into small fragments.

column1: Contrast the genetic similarity of daughter cells in mitosis versus meiosis.
column2: Mitosis produces identical cells; meiosis produces genetically distinct cells.

column1: What occurs during the 'equational division' of meiosis?
column2: Sister chromatids separate, similar to mitosis, but in haploid cells.

column1: What is the purpose of the 'G0' state in animal cells?
column2: A specialized resting state entered when environmental conditions are unfavourable for proliferation.

column1: Identify the protein that acts as a 'molecular brake' at the G1 to S transition by blocking gene expression.
What is the specific role of M-Cdk?
column2: It triggers entry into M phase (mitosis).

column1: During meiosis I, where does the crossover of genetic material specifically occur?
column2: Between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes.

column1: In human meiosis, what is the total number of chromatids present at the start of meiosis I?
What initiates the destruction of M cyclin, leading to the exit from mitosis?
How does cytokinesis in plant cells differ from that in animal cells?
column2: Plant cells form a new cell wall (cell plate) instead of using a contractile ring.

column1: What is the outcome of a single diploid germ-line cell completing the full meiotic cycle?
What structure ensures that duplicated chromosomes are aligned midway between the spindle poles during metaphase?
Which regulatory protein prevents entry into S phase if DNA is damaged?
What is the typical average length of a microtubule during mitosis compared to interphase?
column2: Microtubules are significantly shorter and more dynamic in mitosis (roughly $1\mu m$ vs $20\mu m$).

column1: In humans, which meiotic division results in the formation of polar bodies in females?
column2: Both meiosis I and meiosis II.

column1: What is 'synapsis'?
column2: The tight side-by-side pairing of homologous chromosomes during prophase I.

column1: Why is the interphase between meiosis I and meiosis II unique?
column2: There is no DNA replication during this interphase.

column1: Which mechanism in meiosis ensures that each gamete receives a unique mixture of maternal and paternal chromosomes?
Identify the phase: Nuclear membranes form around 46 chromosomes, and spindle fibers disappear.
What is the consequence of mitotic crossing-over?
column2: It is extremely rare and not a standard feature of the mitotic cycle.

column1: During which phase of mitosis do kinetochore microtubules shorten?
What happens to gene expression when chromosomes condense during mitosis?
Name the protein complex that triggers the transition from metaphase to anaphase.
